notes.summary: A fraction results when a whole number of units is divided into equal parts and shared equally. Each part is called a fractional unit. For example, if three rotis are shared equally among four children, each child gets 3/4 of a roti.
notes.importance: Understanding fractions as equal shares helps in dividing quantities into equal parts and sharing them among individuals.
quick_facts: A fraction represents an equal share of a whole number
Fractional units are equal parts of the whole number
Sharing equally is essential to understand fractions

notes.summary: Fractions that represent the same share or number are called equivalent fractions. They have the same fractional unit, i.e., the same denominator. For example, 3/4, 6/8, and 9/12 are equivalent fractions.
notes.importance: Equivalent fractions help in simplifying complex fraction calculations and comparing quantities.
quick_facts: Equivalent fractions represent the same share or number
They have the same denominator (fractional unit)
To find equivalent fractions, multiply the numerator and denominator by the same number

notes.summary: Brahmagupta's method involves converting fractions into equivalent fractions with the same fractional unit (same denominator). Then, add or subtract the numerators while keeping the same denominator. For example, to add 1/4 and 1/6, convert them to have the same denominator (12) and then add the numerators.
notes.importance: Brahmagupta's method helps in simplifying fraction addition and subtraction by converting fractions into equivalent forms.
quick_facts: Convert fractions to have the same denominator
Add or subtract numerators while keeping the same denominator
This method is essential for adding and subtracting complex fractions
